briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon/evening. We will now begin the climate briefing for May 20, 2026.

sections.title: 🌍 Strengthening the International Community's Resolve to Address Climate Change
sections.content: Today, the international community reaffirmed its strong commitment to addressing climate change. The UN General Assembly overwhelmingly passed a resolution to 'Strengthen Climate Change Measures,' despite opposition from the United States, and the UN Secretary-General welcomed the resolution supporting the International Court of Justice's historic climate crisis ruling [1, 50, 58, 64, 70, 77, 85, 100, 102, 108]. Furthermore, local efforts are continuing, with a city in Oregon, USA, raising $1 billion for climate change response [49]. Global carbon pricing is projected to reach a record high of $107 billion in 2025, demonstrating strengthened financial momentum for climate action [115].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Expanding Domestic Carbon Neutrality Practices and Climate Crisis Adaptation Efforts
sections.content: Domestically, multifaceted efforts to achieve carbon neutrality and adapt to the climate crisis are expanding. Seongbuk-gu is operating a 'Climate Crisis Disaster Response Enhancement Project,' and Jungnang-gu is establishing a culture of environmental action through a resident-participatory 'Carbon Neutrality Challenge' [4, 9, 12, 19, 20, 23, 24, 29]. The Ministry of Climate has initiated technology development utilizing soil as a carbon sink, and regional achievements are also emerging, such as Gyeongsangbuk-do exceeding its greenhouse gas reduction targets [7, 32]. Furthermore, as a debate on amending the Carbon Neutrality Act is held in the National Assembly and legislative discussions on 'carbon neutrality compensation' gain momentum, National Assembly Speaker Woo Won-shik attended the completion ceremony for the National Assembly's solar power generation facility and announced plans to pursue a '2035 Carbon Neutral National Assembly' [14, 15, 27, 28].

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Impacts of Climate Change and International Warnings
sections.content: Globally, severe impacts of climate change and subsequent warnings are emerging. Domestically, Busan City is strengthening its algal bloom warning system in response to the increased risk of green algae due to climate change, and Duke University has warned that heatwaves can lead to multiple health threats, including kidney disease and fungal infections [3, 5]. Internationally, Arctic cultural heritage sites are rapidly being destroyed due to climate change [55, 67, 75, 87], and rivers worldwide are reported to be heating up, becoming polluted, and chemically altered [79, 94]. Furthermore, EU experts have urged the declaration of climate change as a public health emergency [61], and researchers continue to warn about abnormal phenomena such as the potential surge in golf-ball-sized hail and early blooming of roses due to climate change [76, 80, 93, 104, 106]. Amidst these crises, news that Israel's National Security Council has halted climate change work and that the U.S. did not want to mention the term 'climate change' at the G7 suggests difficulties in international cooperation [52, 83].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Facing Challenges
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is steadily accelerating both domestically and internationally. The Korean Society for New and Renewable Energy welcomed the '1st Renewable Energy Master Plan' and emphasized survival strategies for an era of 100 GW renewable energy [16, 34]. The solar industry is shifting from deployment to manufacturing, increasing self-sufficiency by setting a target of 80% domestic modules for 87 GW, and NongHyup Economic Holdings is accelerating efforts to brighten the future of rural areas with new and renewable energy [38, 39, 42, 45]. In the European renewable energy market, competition for battery integration is intensifying [36], and Jeju Island is actively pursuing energy transition experiments to produce green hydrogen from wind power [48]. However, concerns have been raised that the government's renewable energy master plan could directly impact small and medium-sized solar companies, and questions about the feasibility of a plan to create a solar complex the size of 24,000 soccer fields have also emerged [43, 44], indicating the need for a balanced approach.

sections.title: 📰 Discussions on Climate Change Adaptation and Resilience Enhancement
sections.content: Discussions on climate change adaptation and resilience enhancement are emerging as major global topics. The EU Climate Change Adaptation Mission is evaluating adaptation efforts by releasing its 2025/26 activity report [56], and discussions on the necessary financial scale are active, with the UK Climate Change Committee urging £11 billion in annual climate investment [72, 90]. Interest is growing in the wisdom that indigenous communities offer regarding climate change [51], and the latest climate change reports emphasize the importance of supporting vulnerable groups, urging governments to provide technology to help farmers maintain production [91, 107]. Furthermore, there is a growing recognition that storytelling has the power to drive change [82], and successful river restoration cases in UK communities demonstrate local-level efforts to overcome the climate crisis [112].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the international community's solidarity in addressing climate change and the multifaceted efforts both domestically and internationally. At the same time, it underscored the deepening impacts of climate change and the urgent challenges we face. Continued vigilance and proactive action will be crucial moving forward. This concludes today's climate briefing.
